title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: table summarizes recommended measures variable type. PRE = Proportional Reduction Error. measures quantify much knowing one variable reduces prediction error . functions accept contingency table (class table, typically xtabs() table()).

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: assoc_measures() computes available measures : useful exploratory analysis. reporting, pick measure matches variable types.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) assoc_measures(tbl) #> Measure                            Estimate     SE  CI lower  CI upper        p  #> Cramer's V                            0.136     --     0.079     0.191  < 0.001  #> Contingency Coefficient               0.134     --        --        --  < 0.001  #> Lambda symmetric                      0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000       --  #> Lambda R|C                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000       --  #> Lambda C|R                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000       --  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C             0.018  0.008     0.003     0.034    0.023  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R             0.008  0.003     0.001     0.014    0.022  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric     0.011  0.005     0.002     0.021    0.021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C           0.018  0.008     0.003     0.032    0.021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R           0.009  0.004     0.001     0.016    0.021  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                -0.268  0.056    -0.378    -0.158  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                      -0.126  0.027    -0.180    -0.073  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                      -0.117  0.026    -0.167    -0.067  < 0.001  #> Somers' D R|C                        -0.091  0.020    -0.131    -0.052  < 0.001  #> Somers' D C|R                        -0.175  0.038    -0.249    -0.101  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Cramer’s V measures strength association two nominal variables. ranges 0 (association) 1 (perfect association). Pass detail = TRUE confidence interval p-value. p-value tests null hypothesis association using Pearson chi-squared test.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) cramer_v(tbl) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: 2x2 tables, Phi equivalent Cramer’s V. Unlike V, Phi can negative table 2x2, indicating direction association. p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) phi(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.006     0.000     0.063  0.839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: contingency coefficient alternative Cramer’s V. upper bound depends table dimensions, makes harder compare across tables different sizes. p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: contingency_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.134        --        --  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: variables ordinal (ordered factors), measures account ordering appropriate Cramer’s V.

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Gamma ranges -1 +1. ignores tied pairs, makes sensitive direction association tends overestimate strength many ties. positive value means higher values one variable tend occur higher values . p-value tests H0: Gamma = 0 using Wald z-test.
code: tbl_ord <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) gamma_gk(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.310     0.238     0.383  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau-b adjusts ties ranges -1 +1. generally preferred Gamma square near-square tables. p-value tests H0: Tau-b = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_b(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.205     0.155     0.254  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau-c similar Tau-b adjusts rectangular tables number rows columns differ. p-value tests H0: Tau-c = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_c(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.200     0.151     0.248  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Somers’ D asymmetric measure: distinguishes dependent independent variable. default, row variable treated dependent (D(R|C)). p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: somers_d(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.208     0.157     0.258  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: measures answer specific question: much knowing column variable reduce error predicting row variable (vice versa)?

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Lambda measures proportional reduction classification error. can equal zero even variables associated, modal category change across columns. p-value tests H0: Lambda = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) lambda_gk(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.012     0.000     0.039  0.389

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau measures proportional reduction error predicting row variable column variable, using full distribution (just mode). p-value tests H0: Tau = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.017     0.008     0.026  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: uncertainty coefficient (Theil’s U) based entropy. measures much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty . p-value tests H0: U = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: uncertainty_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.028     0.016     0.040  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Yule’s Q defined 2x2 tables . ranges -1 +1 equivalent Gamma 2x2 tables. p-value tests H0: Q = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) yule_q(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.015    -0.126     0.155  0.839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: cross_tab() can automatically select appropriate measure via assoc_measure = "auto" (default). variables ordered factors, picks Kendall’s Tau-b; otherwise uses Cramer’s V. can override automatic choice:

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: functions support confidence intervals via detail = TRUE. confidence level defaults 95% can changed conf_level: get estimate p-value (CI), pass conf_level = NULL:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = 0.99) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.176     0.103     0.248  < 0.001 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #> Estimate        p #>    0.176  < 0.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: detail = FALSE (default), functions return plain numeric scalar, R’s formatting rules apply. detail = TRUE, result uses custom print method defaults 3 decimal places. Pass digits change (p-value always uses 3 decimal places < 0.001): digits argument works assoc_measures(): can also store result re-display different precision without recalculating:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, digits = 4) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>   0.1762    0.1203    0.2309  < 0.001 assoc_measures(tbl, digits = 2) #> Measure                            Estimate    SE  CI lower  CI upper        p  #> Cramer's V                             0.18    --      0.12      0.23  < 0.001  #> Contingency Coefficient                0.24    --        --        --  < 0.001  #> Lambda symmetric                       0.01  0.01      0.00      0.04    0.389  #> Lambda R|C                             0.00  0.00      0.00      0.00       --  #> Lambda C|R                             0.02  0.03      0.00      0.07    0.386  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C              0.02  0.00      0.01      0.03  < 0.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R              0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  < 0.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric      0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  < 0.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C            0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  < 0.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R            0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  < 0.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                  0.31  0.04      0.24      0.38  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                        0.20  0.03      0.16      0.25  < 0.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                        0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  < 0.001  #> Somers' D R|C                          0.21  0.03      0.16      0.26  < 0.001  #> Somers' D C|R                          0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  < 0.001 res <- cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) print(res, digits = 5) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>  0.17617   0.12031   0.23092  < 0.001

title: Getting started with spicy
text: See ?varlist inspect variables, labels, values, missing data. See ?cross_tab full list arguments (weights, simulation, association measures). See ?table_categorical grouped one-way categorical tables. See ?table_continuous continuous summaries group comparisons. See ?assoc_measures complete list association statistics. See ?code_book generate interactive HTML codebook.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: Use function matches type variables want report: practice: use table_categorical() smoking status, education, activity; use table_continuous() BMI, income, scale scores; keep grouping variable want compare across.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: functions use core arguments: argument pattern works cases: select chooses reported variables; defines grouping structure; labels cleans row labels; output decides result rendered exported.
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" ) table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   labels = c(     bmi = "Body mass index",     wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",     life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"   ),   output = "tinytable" )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: common report contains table types, often grouping variable. example, might first summarize categorical health behaviors, summarize continuous well-indicators.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),     by = education,     labels = c(       "Smoking status",       "Regular physical activity",       "Visited a dentist in the last 12 months"     ),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: keeps reporting structure consistent still using function fits variable type.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     labels = c(       bmi = "Body mass index",       wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",       life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"     ),     p_value = TRUE,     effect_size = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: functions support reporting formats: Pick output based table going, analysis . underlying selection grouping pattern stays . want object fits naturally Word PowerPoint workflows can also rendered HTML documents, flextable good choice:
code: if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   ) }

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: summary-table helpers return regular gt, tinytable, flextable objects, can keep styling native package API. Use gt:: functions want keep gt workflow: Use tinytable:: functions want lightweight table-specific styling: Use flextable:: functions want keep working toward Office HTML document output. example shown code dark pkgdown theme reliable preview final flextable HTML rendering:
code: tab <- pkgdown_dark_gt(table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "gt" ))  tab |>   gt::tab_header(     title = "Health behaviors by education",     subtitle = "Categorical summary table"   ) |>   gt::tab_source_note(     gt::md("*Percentages are computed within each education group.*")   ) tab <- table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" )  tab |>   tinytable::style_tt(     i = 2:3,     j = 2:5,     background = "red",     color = "white",     bold = TRUE   ) if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   tab <- table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   )    tab |>     flextable::theme_booktabs() |>     flextable::autofit() |>     flextable::fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") }

what: Keep the detailed options in the function-specific articles
title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: dedicated articles go deeper function: table_categorical() covers missing values, level filtering, association measures, one-way frequency-style tables. table_continuous() covers grouped descriptive statistics, parametric nonparametric tests, effect sizes. Use vignette final reporting overview, consult function-specific articles need detailed controls.

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: grouped tables, provide data frame, one selected variables, grouping variable: default output "default", prints styled ASCII table console. Use output = "data.frame" get plain numeric data frame suitable processing.

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Omit build frequency-style table selected variables:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   output = "default" ) #> Categorical table #>  #>  Variable               │        n          %  #> ────────────────────────┼───────────────────── #>  smoking                │                      #>    No                   │      926       78.8  #>    Yes                  │      249       21.2  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity      │                      #>    No                   │      650       54.2  #>    Yes                  │      550       45.8

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: table_categorical() supports several output formats. table summarizes options:

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: "gt" format produces table APA-style borders, column spanners, proper alignment:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),     by = education,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = sex,   output = "tinytable" )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Use output = "data.frame" wide numeric data frame (one row per modality), output = "long" long format (one row per modality x group):
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2  smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V #> 1  0.1356677 #> 2  0.1356677

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, table_categorical() uses variable names row headers. Use labels argument provide human-readable labels:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, table_categorical() reports Cramer’s V nominal variables automatically switches Kendall’s Tau-b variables ordered factors. Override assoc_measure: Add confidence intervals assoc_ci = TRUE. rendered formats (gt, tinytable, flextable), CI shown inline: data formats ("data.frame", "long", "excel", "clipboard"), separate CI lower CI upper columns added:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_measure = "lambda",   output = "tinytable" ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     assoc_ci = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_ci = TRUE,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2  smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V   CI lower  CI upper #> 1  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716 #> 2  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Pass survey weights weights argument. Use rescale = TRUE total weighted N matches unweighted N:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     weights = "weight",     rescale = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, rows missing values dropped (drop_na = TRUE). Set drop_na = FALSE display “(Missing)” category:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Use levels_keep display specific modalities. order specify controls display order, useful placing “(Missing)” first highlight missingness:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     levels_keep = c("(Missing)", "Low", "High"),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Control number digits percentages, p-values, association measure:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = smoking,     by = education,     percent_digits = 2,     p_digits = 4,     v_digits = 3,     output = "gt"   ) )

what: Exporting to Excel, Word, or clipboard
title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Excel export, provide file path: Word, use output = "word": can also copy directly clipboard pasting spreadsheet text editor:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "excel",   excel_path = "my_table.xlsx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "word",   word_path = "my_table.docx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   output = "clipboard" )

title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: Add summarize variables across categories: main pattern reporting continuous variables across groups education, sex, treatment arm, survey wave.

title: spicy: frequency tables, cross-tabulations, and summary tables in R
text: spicy helps explore categorical, quantitative, numeric data R, tools labelled variables, frequency tables, cross-tabulations, summary tables. provides readable, console-first outputs. designed survey research, descriptive statistics, reporting workflows, tools frequency tables, cross-tabulations chi-squared tests effect sizes, categorical continuous summary tables, variable inspection, codebooks. spicy, can: Inspect variables varlist() vl() names, labels, values, classes, missing data. Create frequency tables R freq(). Create cross-tabulations R cross_tab(), including percentages, chi-squared tests, effect sizes. Measure associations cramer_v(), phi(), gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b(), somers_d(), related functions. Build categorical summary tables R table_categorical() gt, tinytable, flextable, Excel, Word, clipboard export. Build continuous summary tables R table_continuous() console, gt, tinytable, flextable, Excel, Word, clipboard output. Generate codebooks code_book() labelled survey-style datasets. Extract variable labels label_from_names(), including LimeSurvey-style headers. Works labelled, factor, ordered, Date, POSIXct, common variable types. full introduction, see Getting started spicy.

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: x contingency table (class table). type family measures compute: "" (default), "nominal", "ordinal". conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3).

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: data frame columns measure, estimate, se, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value. nominal measures (Cramer's V, Phi, Contingency Coef.), p-value comes Pearson chi-squared test independence. measures, Wald z-test H0: measure = 0.

title: Association measures summary table — assoc_measures
text: type = "" (default) returns nominal ordinal measures. Use type = "nominal" type = "ordinal" restrict output single family. nominal family includes cramer_v(), contingency_coef(), lambda_gk(), goodman_kruskal_tau(), uncertainty_coef(), (2x2 tables) phi() yule_q(). ordinal family includes gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b(), kendall_tau_c(), somers_d(). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024).

what: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: Low-level internal function constructs visually aligned ASCII table data.frame. supports Unicode characters, ANSI colors, dynamic width adjustment, left/right alignment, spacing control. function primarily used internally higher-level wrappers spicy_print_table() print.spicy_freq_table().

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
code: build_ascii_table(   x,   padding = c("compact", "normal", "wide"),   first_column_line = TRUE,   row_total_line = TRUE,   column_total_line = TRUE,   bottom_line = FALSE,   lines_color = "darkgrey",   align_left_cols = c(1L, 2L),   align_center_cols = integer(0),   group_sep_rows = integer(0),   ... )

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: x data.frame spicy_table object containing table format. Typically, includes columns Category, Values, Freq., Percent, etc. padding Character string controlling horizontal spacing columns: "compact" — minimal spacing "normal" — moderate spacing (default) "wide" — extra spacing (large displays wide content) first_column_line Logical. TRUE (default), vertical separator drawn first column (useful separating categories data). row_total_line, column_total_line Logical. Control horizontal rules total rows columns. default TRUE. bottom_line Logical. FALSE (default), closing line drawn. TRUE, draws closing line bottom table. lines_color Character. Color used table separators. Defaults "darkgrey". color applied ANSI color support available (see crayon::has_color()). align_left_cols Integer vector column indices left-align. Defaults c(1, 2) frequency tables (Category + Values). align_center_cols Integer vector column indices center-align. Defaults integer(0) (centered columns). Columns align_left_cols align_center_cols right-aligned. group_sep_rows Integer vector row indices light dashed separator line drawn. Defaults integer(0). ... Additional arguments (currently ignored).

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: single character string containing full ASCII-formatted table, suitable direct printing cat().

title: Build a formatted ASCII table — build_ascii_table
text: build_ascii_table() rendering engine produces aligned text layout spicy-formatted tables. automatically detects cell widths (including colored text), inserts Unicode separators, applies padding different display modes ("compact", "normal", "wide"). users, function called directly. Instead, use spicy_print_table() adds headers, notes, alignment logic automatically.

Percent  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────── #>  Valid    │ Yes        12     57.1  #>           │ No          8     38.1  #>  Missing  │ NA          1      4.8  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────── #>  Total    │            21      100

what: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: code_book() creates interactive exportable codebook summarizing variables data frame. builds upon varlist() provide overview variable names, labels, classes, representative values sortable, searchable table. output displayed interactive DT::datatable() Viewer pane, allowing filtering, column reordering, export (copy, print, CSV, Excel, PDF) directly.

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
code: code_book(x, values = FALSE, include_na = FALSE, title = "Codebook", ...)

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: x data frame tibble. values Logical. FALSE (default), displays compact summary variable's values. numeric, character, date/time, labelled, factor variables, four unique non-missing values shown: first three values, followed ellipsis (...), last value. Values sorted appropriate (e.g., numeric, character, date) factors, levels used directly sorted. labelled variables, prefixed labels displayed via labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed"). TRUE, unique non-missing values displayed. include_na Logical. TRUE, unique missing values (NA, NaN) explicitly appended end Values summary present variable. applies variable types. FALSE (default), missing values omitted Values still counted NAs column. title Optional character string displayed table title Viewer. Defaults "Codebook". Set NULL remove title completely. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Generate an interactive variable codebook — code_book
text: DT::datatable object.

what: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: count_n() counts, row data frame matrix, many times one values appear across selected columns. supports type-safe comparison, case-insensitive string matching, detection special values NA, NaN, Inf, -Inf.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
code: count_n(   data = NULL,   select = tidyselect::everything(),   exclude = NULL,   count = NULL,   special = NULL,   allow_coercion = TRUE,   ignore_case = FALSE,   regex = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: data data frame matrix. Optional inside mutate(). select Columns include. Defaults tidyselect::everything(). Uses tidyselect helpers like tidyselect::starts_with(), etc. regex = TRUE, select treated regex string. exclude Character vector column names exclude selection. Defaults NULL (exclusion). count Value(s) count. Defaults NULL. Ignored special used. Multiple values allowed (e.g., count = c(1, 2, 3) count = c("yes", "")). R automatically coerces values count common type (e.g., c(2, "2") becomes c("2", "2")), values expected final type. allow_coercion = FALSE, matching type-safe using identical(), type count must match values data. special Character vector special values count: "NA", "NaN", "Inf", "-Inf", "". Defaults NULL. "NA" uses .na(), therefore includes NA NaN values. "NaN" uses .nan() match actual NaN values. allow_coercion Logical. TRUE (default), values compared coercion. FALSE, uses strict matching via identical(). ignore_case Logical. FALSE (default), comparisons case-sensitive. TRUE, performs case-insensitive string comparisons. regex Logical. FALSE (default), uses tidyselect helpers. TRUE, interprets select regular expression pattern. verbose Logical. FALSE (default), messages suppressed. TRUE, prints processing messages.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: numeric vector row-wise counts (unnamed).

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: function particularly useful summarizing data quality patterns row-wise structures, designed work fluently inside dplyr::mutate() pipelines. Internally, count_n() wraps stable dependency-free base function base_count_n(), allowing high flexibility testability.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: function inspired datawizard::row_count(), provides additional flexibility: Element-wise type-safe matching using identical() allow_coercion = FALSE. ensures value type match exactly, enabling precise comparisons mixed-type columns. Support multiple values count, allowing queries like count = c(2, 3) count = c("yes", "") count several values per row. Detection special values NA, NaN, Inf, -Inf special argument — feature available row_count(). Tidyverse-native behavior: can used inside mutate() without explicitly passing data argument.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: R automatically coerces mixed-type vectors passed count common type. example, count = c(2, "2") becomes c("2", "2"), R converts numeric character values unified type. means mixed-type checks possible runtime count passed function. ensure accurate type-sensitive matching, users avoid mixing types count explicitly.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: strict matching enabled, value count must match type target column exactly. factor columns, means count must also factor. Supplying count = "b" (character string) match factor value, even label appears identical. common intuitive approach use count = factor("b"), works many cases. However, identical() — used internally strict comparisons — also checks internal structure factor, including order content levels. result, comparisons may still fail levels differ, even label . ensure perfect match (label levels), can reuse value taken directly data (e.g., df$x[2]). guarantees class factor levels align. However, approach works reliably selected columns factor structure.

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: ignore_case = TRUE, values involved comparison converted lowercase using tolower() matching. behavior applies character factor columns. Factors first converted character internally. Importantly, case-insensitive mode takes precedence strict type comparison: values longer compared using identical(), rather using lowercase string equality. enables flexible matching — example, "b" "B" match even allow_coercion = FALSE.

what: Example: strict vs. case-insensitive matching with factors
title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
text: Like datawizard::row_count(), function also supports regex-based column selection, case-insensitive string comparison, column exclusion.
code: df <- tibble::tibble(   x = factor(c("a", "b", "c")),   y = factor(c("b", "B", "a")) )  # Strict match fails with character input count_n(df, count = "b", allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Match works only where factor levels match exactly count_n(df, count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)), allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0  # Case-insensitive match succeeds for both "b" and "B" count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 0

title: Row-wise Count of Specific or Special Values — count_n
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(tibble) library(haven)  # Basic usage df <- tibble(   x = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   y = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   z = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2") ) df #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>       x     y z     #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr> #> 1     1     2 2     #> 2     2     2 2     #> 3     2    NA 2     #> 4     3     3 3     #> 5    NA     2 2     count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, count = 2, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, count = "2", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 df |> mutate(num_twos = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>       x     y z     num_twos #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr>    <dbl> #> 1     1     2 2            2 #> 2     2     2 2            3 #> 3     2    NA 2            2 #> 4     3     3 3            0 #> 5    NA     2 2            2  # Mixed types and special values df <- tibble(   num   = c(1, 2, NA, -Inf, NaN),   char  = c("a", "B", "b", "a", NA),   fact  = factor(c("a", "b", "b", "a", "c")),   date  = as.Date(c("2023-01-01", "2023-01-01", NA, "2023-01-02", "2023-01-01")),   lab   = labelled(c(1, 2, 1, 2, NA), labels = c(No = 1, Yes = 2)),   logic = c(TRUE, FALSE, NA, TRUE, FALSE) ) df #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>     num char  fact  date       lab       logic #>   <dbl> <chr> <fct> <date>     <dbl+lbl> <lgl> #> 1     1 a     a     2023-01-01  1 [No]   TRUE  #> 2     2 B     b     2023-01-01  2 [Yes]  FALSE #> 3    NA b     b     NA          1 [No]   NA    #> 4  -Inf a     a     2023-01-02  2 [Yes]  TRUE  #> 5   NaN NA    c     2023-01-01 NA        FALSE count_n(df, count = 2) #> [1] 0 2 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = 2, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0 0 0 count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 2 0 0 count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 0 2 2 0 0 count_n(df, count = "a", select = fact) #> [1] 1 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = as.Date("2023-01-01"), select = date) #> [1] 1 1 0 0 1 count_n(df, count = TRUE, select = logic) #> [1] 1 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = 2, select = lab) #> [1] 0 1 0 1 0 df <- df |> mutate(lab_chr = as_factor(lab)) count_n(df, count = "Yes", select = lab_chr, allow_coercion = TRUE) #> [1] 0 1 0 1 0 count_n(df, count = "Yes", select = lab_chr, allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0 0 0  # Count special values count_n(df, special = "NA") #> [1] 0 0 3 0 4 count_n(df, special = "NaN") #> [1] 0 0 0 0 1 count_n(df, special = "-Inf") #> [1] 0 0 0 1 0 count_n(df, special = c("NA", "NaN")) #> [1] 0 0 3 0 4 count_n(df, special = "all") #> [1] 0 0 3 1 4  # Column selection strategies df <- tibble(   score_math    = c(1, 2, 2, 3, NA),   score_science = c(2, 2, NA, 3, 2),   score_lang    = c("2", "2", "2", "3", "2"),   name          = c("Jean", "Marie", "Ali", "Zoe", "Nina") ) df #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>   score_math score_science score_lang name  #>        <dbl>         <dbl> <chr>      <chr> #> 1          1             2 2          Jean  #> 2          2             2 2          Marie #> 3          2            NA 2          Ali   #> 4          3             3 3          Zoe   #> 5         NA             2 2          Nina  count_n(df, select = c(score_math, score_science), count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = starts_with("score_"), exclude = "score_lang", count = 2) #> [1] 1 2 1 0 1 count_n(df, select = everything(), exclude = "name", count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, select = "^score_", regex = TRUE, count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2 0 2 count_n(df, select = "lang", regex = TRUE, count = "2") #> [1] 1 1 1 0 1 df |> mutate(nb_two = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   score_math score_science score_lang name  nb_two #>        <dbl>         <dbl> <chr>      <chr>  <dbl> #> 1          1             2 2          Jean       2 #> 2          2             2 2          Marie      3 #> 3          2            NA 2          Ali        2 #> 4          3             3 3          Zoe        0 #> 5         NA             2 2          Nina       2 df |>   select(score_math, score_science) |>   mutate(nb_two = count_n(count = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   score_math score_science nb_two #>        <dbl>         <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1          1             2      1 #> 2          2             2      2 #> 3          2            NA      1 #> 4          3             3      0 #> 5         NA             2      1 df$nb_two <- count_n(df, select = starts_with("score_"), count = 2) df[1:3, ] |> count_n(select = starts_with("score_"), count = 2) #> [1] 2 3 2  # Strict type-safe matching with factor columns df <- tibble(   x = factor(c("a", "b", "c")),   y = factor(c("b", "B", "a")) ) df #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   x     y     #>   <fct> <fct> #> 1 a     b     #> 2 b     B     #> 3 c     a      # Coercion: character "b" matches both x and y count_n(df, count = "b") #> [1] 1 1 0  # Strict match: fails because "b" is character, not factor (returns only 0s) count_n(df, count = "b", allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Strict match with factor value: works only where levels match count_n(df, count = factor("b", levels = levels(df$x)), allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0  # Using a value from the data: guarantees type and levels match for column x count_n(df, count = df$x[2], allow_coercion = FALSE) #> [1] 0 1 0  # Case-insensitive match (factors are converted to character internally) count_n(df, count = "b", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 0 count_n(df, count = "B", ignore_case = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 0

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: cramer_v() computes Cramer's V two-way contingency table, measuring strength association two categorical variables.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
code: cramer_v(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: detail = FALSE: single numeric value (estimate). detail = TRUE conf_level non-NULL: c(estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value). detail = TRUE conf_level = NULL: c(estimate, p_value). p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test).

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Cramer's V computed \(V = \sqrt{\chi^2 / (n \cdot (k - 1))}\), \(\chi^2\) Pearson chi-squared statistic, \(n\) total count, \(k = \min(r, c)\). confidence interval uses Fisher z-transformation. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024).

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
text: Agresti, . (2002). Categorical Data Analysis (2nd ed.). Wiley. Liebetrau, . M. (1983). Measures Association. Sage. Signorell, . et al. (2024). DescTools: Tools Descriptive Statistics. R package.

title: Cramer's V — cramer_v
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) cramer_v(tab) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tab, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  < 0.001 cramer_v(tab, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #> Estimate        p #>    0.136  < 0.001

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: Computes two-way cross-tabulation optional weights, grouping (including combinations multiple variables), percentage displays, inferential statistics. cross_tab() produces weighted unweighted contingency tables row column percentages, optional grouping via , associated Chi-squared tests association measure diagnostic information. x y variables required. one-way frequency tables, use freq() instead.

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
code: cross_tab(   data,   x,   y = NULL,   by = NULL,   weights = NULL,   rescale = FALSE,   percent = c("none", "column", "row"),   include_stats = TRUE,   assoc_measure = c("auto", "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d",     "lambda", "none"),   assoc_ci = FALSE,   correct = FALSE,   simulate_p = FALSE,   simulate_B = 2000,   digits = NULL,   styled = TRUE,   show_n = TRUE )  # S3 method for class 'spicy_cross_table_list' print(x, ...)

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: data data frame. Alternatively, vector using vector-based interface. x Row variable (unquoted). y Column variable (unquoted). Mandatory; one-way tables, use freq(). Optional grouping variable expression. Can single variable combination multiple variables (e.g. interaction(vs, )). weights Optional numeric weights. rescale Logical. FALSE (default), weights used -. TRUE, rescales weights total weighted N matches raw N. percent One "none" (default), "row", "column". Unique abbreviations accepted (e.g. "n", "r", "c"). include_stats Logical. TRUE (default), computes Chi-squared association measure (see assoc_measure). assoc_measure Character. association measure report. "auto" (default) selects Kendall's Tau-b variables ordered factors Cramer's V otherwise. choices: "cramer_v", "phi", "gamma", "tau_b", "tau_c", "somers_d", "lambda", "none". assoc_ci Logical. TRUE, includes 95 percent confidence interval association measure note. Defaults FALSE. correct Logical. FALSE (default), continuity correction applied. TRUE, applies Yates correction (2x2 tables). simulate_p Logical. FALSE (default), uses asymptotic p-values. TRUE, uses Monte Carlo simulation. simulate_B Integer. Number replicates Monte Carlo simulation. Defaults 2000. digits Number decimals. Defaults 1 percentages, 0 counts. styled Logical. TRUE (default), returns spicy_cross_table object (formatted printing). FALSE, returns plain data.frame. show_n Logical. TRUE (default), adds marginal N totals percent != "none". ... Additional arguments passed individual print methods.

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: data.frame, list data.frames, spicy_cross_table object. used, returns spicy_cross_table_list.

title: Cross-tabulation — cross_tab
text: function recognizes following global options modify default behavior: options(spicy.percent = "column") Sets default percentage mode calls cross_tab(). Valid values "none", "row", "column". Equivalent setting percent = "column" (another choice) call. options(spicy.simulate_p = TRUE) Enables Monte Carlo simulation Chi-squared tests default. Equivalent setting simulate_p = TRUE every call. options(spicy.rescale = TRUE) Automatically rescales weights total weighted N equals raw N. Equivalent setting rescale = TRUE call. options convenient users wish enforce consistent behavior across multiple calls cross_tab() spicy table functions. can disabled reset setting NULL: options(spicy.percent = NULL, spicy.simulate_p = NULL, spicy.rescale = NULL). Example:
code: options(spicy.simulate_p = TRUE, spicy.rescale = TRUE) cross_tab(sochealth, smoking, education, weights = weight)

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: Creates frequency table vector variable data frame, options weighting, sorting, handling labelled data, defining custom missing values, displaying cumulative percentages. styled = TRUE, function prints spicy-formatted ASCII table using print.spicy_freq_table() spicy_print_table(); otherwise, returns data.frame containing frequencies proportions.

title: Frequency Table — freq
code: freq(   data,   x = NULL,   weights = NULL,   digits = 1,   valid = TRUE,   cum = FALSE,   sort = "",   na_val = NULL,   labelled_levels = c("prefixed", "labels", "values", "p", "l", "v"),   rescale = TRUE,   styled = TRUE,   ... )

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: data data.frame, vector, factor. data frame provided, specify target variable x. x variable data (unquoted). weights Optional numeric vector weights (length x). variable may referenced bare name belongs data. digits Number decimal digits display percentages (default: 1). valid Logical. TRUE (default), display valid percentages (excluding missing values). cum Logical. FALSE (default), cumulative percentages omitted. TRUE, adds cumulative percentages. sort Sorting method values: "" - sorting (default) "+" - increasing frequency "-" - decreasing frequency "name+" - alphabetical -Z "name-" - alphabetical Z-na_val Vector numeric character values treated missing (NA). labelled variables (haven labelled), argument must refer underlying coded values, visible labels. Example:   labelled_levels labelled variables, defines labels values displayed: "prefixed" "p" - show labels [value] label (default) "labels" "l" - show labels "values" "v" - show numeric codes rescale Logical. TRUE (default), rescale weights total equals unweighted sample size. styled Logical. TRUE (default), print formatted spicy table. FALSE, return plain data.frame frequency values. ... Additional arguments passed print.spicy_freq_table().
code: x <- labelled(c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3), c("Low" = 1, "Medium" = 2, "High" = 3)) freq(x, na_val = 1) # Treat all "Low" as missing

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: data.frame columns: value - unique values factor levels n - frequency count (weighted applicable) prop - proportion total valid_prop - proportion valid responses (valid = TRUE) cum_prop, cum_valid_prop - cumulative percentages (cum = TRUE) styled = TRUE, prints formatted table console returns invisibly.

title: Frequency Table — freq
text: function designed mimic common frequency procedures statistical software SPSS Stata, integrating flexibility R's data structures. automatically detects type input (vector, factor, labelled) applies appropriate transformations, including: Handling labelled variables via labelled haven Optional recoding specific values missing (na_val) Optional weighting rescaling mechanism Support cumulative percentages (cum = TRUE) Multiple display modes labels via labelled_levels weighting applied (weights), frequencies percentages computed proportionally weights. argument rescale = TRUE normalizes weights sum equals unweighted sample size.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: gamma_gk() computes Goodman-Kruskal Gamma statistic two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: gamma_gk(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: gamma = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
text: Gamma computed \(\gamma = (C - D) / (C + D)\), \(C\) \(D\) numbers concordant discordant pairs. ignores tied pairs, making appropriate ordinal variables many ties. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal Gamma — gamma_gk
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) gamma_gk(tab) #> [1] 0.3104791 gamma_gk(tab, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.310     0.238     0.383  < 0.001

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: goodman_kruskal_tau() computes Goodman-Kruskal's Tau, proportional reduction error (PRE) measure nominal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(   x,   direction = c("row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "row" (default, column predicts row) "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
text: Unlike lambda_gk(), Goodman-Kruskal's Tau uses cell frequencies rather modal categories, making sensitive association patterns lambda may zero. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Tau — goodman_kruskal_tau
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) goodman_kruskal_tau(tab) #> [1] 0.01840572 goodman_kruskal_tau(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.008     0.001     0.014  0.022

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: kendall_tau_b() computes Kendall's Tau-b two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
code: kendall_tau_b(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau-b = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
text: Kendall's Tau-b computed \(\tau_b = (C - D) / \sqrt{(n_0 - n_1)(n_0 - n_2)}\), \(n_0 = n(n-1)/2\), \(n_1\) number pairs tied row variable, \(n_2\) number tied column variable. Tau-b corrects ties appropriate square tables. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Kendall's Tau-b — kendall_tau_b
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) kendall_tau_b(tab) #> [1] 0.2045524

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: kendall_tau_c() computes Stuart's Tau-c (also known Kendall's Tau-c) two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
code: kendall_tau_c(   x,   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: tau-c = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
text: Stuart's Tau-c computed \(\tau_c = 2m(C - D) / (n^2(m - 1))\), \(m = \min(r, c)\). appropriate rectangular tables restricted range \([-1, 1]\) square tables. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Kendall's Tau-c (Stuart's Tau-c) — kendall_tau_c
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) kendall_tau_c(tab) #> [1] 0.1996409

what: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: Splits column name first occurrence sep, renames column part sep (name), assigns part sep labelled::var_label(). works even label contains separator.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
code: label_from_names(df, sep = ". ")

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: df data.frame tibble column names form "name<sep>label" (e.g. "name. label"). (default LimeSurvey). sep Character string used separator name label. Default ". " (LimeSurvey's default), literal string can used.

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: base tibble column names equal names (sep) var_label attributes equal labels (sep).

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
text: function especially useful LimeSurvey CSV exports using Export results → Export format: CSV → Headings: Question code & question text, column names look like "code. question text". case default separator ". ".

title: Derive variable labels from column names name<sep>label — label_from_names
code: # Example with LimeSurvey-style column names df <- data.frame(   "age. Age of respondent" = c(25, 30),   "score. Total score. Manually computed." = c(12, 14),   check.names = FALSE )  # sep = ". " by default (LimeSurvey) out <- label_from_names(df) labelled::var_label(out) #> $age #> [1] "Age of respondent" #>  #> $score #> [1] "Total score. Manually computed." #>   # Example with a custom separator ("|") df2 <- data.frame(   "id|Identifier" = 1:3,   "score|Total score" = c(10, 20, 30),   check.names = FALSE ) out2 <- label_from_names(df2, sep = "|") labelled::var_label(out2) #> $id #> [1] "Identifier" #>  #> $score #> [1] "Total score" #>

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: lambda_gk() computes Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda, proportional reduction error (PRE) measure nominal variables.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
code: lambda_gk(   x,   direction = c("symmetric", "row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "symmetric" (default), "row" (column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: lambda = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
text: Lambda measures much prediction error reduced independent variable used predict dependent variable. ranges 0 (reduction) 1 (perfect prediction). Lambda can equal zero even variables associated modal category dominates every column (row). Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Goodman-Kruskal's Lambda — lambda_gk
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) lambda_gk(tab) #> [1] 0 lambda_gk(tab, direction = "row") #> [1] 0 lambda_gk(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper   p #>    0.000     0.000     0.000  --

what: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
text: mean_n() computes row means data.frame matrix, handling missing values (NAs) automatically. Row-wise means calculated across selected numeric columns, optional condition minimum number (proportion) valid (non-missing) values required row included. Non-numeric columns excluded automatically reported.

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
code: mean_n(   data = NULL,   select = dplyr::everything(),   exclude = NULL,   min_valid = NULL,   digits = NULL,   regex = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
text: data data.frame matrix. select Columns include. regex = FALSE, use tidyselect syntax (default: dplyr::everything()). regex = TRUE, provide regular expression pattern (character string). exclude Columns exclude (default: NULL). min_valid Minimum number valid (non-NA) values required per row. proportion, applied number selected columns. Defaults NULL (values must valid). digits Optional number decimal places round result. Defaults NULL (rounding). regex Logical. FALSE (default), uses tidyselect helpers. TRUE, select argument treated regular expression. verbose Logical. FALSE (default), messages suppressed. TRUE, prints message non-numeric columns excluded.

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
text: numeric vector row-wise means.

title: Row Means with Optional Minimum Valid Values — mean_n
code: library(dplyr)  # Create a simple numeric data frame df <- tibble(   var1 = c(10, NA, 30, 40, 50),   var2 = c(5, NA, 15, NA, 25),   var3 = c(NA, 30, 20, 50, 10) )  # Compute row-wise mean (all values must be valid by default) mean_n(df) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Require at least 2 valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 2) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Require at least 50% valid (non-NA) values per row mean_n(df, min_valid = 0.5) #> [1]  7.50000       NA 21.66667 45.00000 28.33333  # Round the result to 1 decimal mean_n(df, digits = 1) #> [1]   NA   NA 21.7   NA 28.3  # Select specific columns mean_n(df, select = c(var1, var2)) #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select specific columns using a pipe df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mean_n() #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Exclude a column mean_n(df, exclude = "var3") #> [1]  7.5   NA 22.5   NA 37.5  # Select columns ending with "1" mean_n(df, select = ends_with("1")) #> [1] 10 NA 30 40 50  # Use with native pipe df |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use inside dplyr::mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Select columns directly inside mutate() df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = c(var1, var2), min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       22.5 #> 4    40    NA    50       40   #> 5    50    25    10       37.5  # Select columns before mutate df |>   select(var1, var2) |>   mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 1)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>    var1  var2 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA       NA   #> 3    30    15       22.5 #> 4    40    NA       40   #> 5    50    25       37.5  # Show verbose processing info df |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(min_valid = 2, digits = 1, verbose = TRUE)) #> mean_n(): Row means computed with min_valid = 2, regex = FALSE #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>    var1  var2  var3 mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA        7.5 #> 2    NA    NA    30       NA   #> 3    30    15    20       21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50       45   #> 5    50    25    10       28.3  # Add character and grouping columns df_mixed <- mutate(df,   name = letters[1:5],   group = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "A") ) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A     #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A     #> 3    30    15    20 c     B     #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B     #> 5    50    25    10 e     A      # Non-numeric columns are ignored mean_n(df_mixed) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Use within mutate() on mixed data df_mixed |> mutate(mean_score = mean_n(select = starts_with("var"))) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # Use everything() but exclude non-numeric columns manually mean_n(df_mixed, select = everything(), exclude = "group") #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Select columns using regex mean_n(df_mixed, select = "^var", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333 mean_n(df_mixed, select = "ar", regex = TRUE) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667       NA 28.33333  # Apply to a subset of rows (first 3) df_mixed[1:3, ] |> mean_n(select = starts_with("var")) #> [1]       NA       NA 21.66667  # Store the result in a new column df_mixed$mean_score <- mean_n(df_mixed, select = starts_with("var")) df_mixed #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>    var1  var2  var3 name  group mean_score #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1    10     5    NA a     A           NA   #> 2    NA    NA    30 b     A           NA   #> 3    30    15    20 c     B           21.7 #> 4    40    NA    50 d     B           NA   #> 5    50    25    10 e     A           28.3  # With a numeric matrix mat <- matrix(c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5, NA, 7, 8, 9), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) mat #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    1    2   NA #> [2,]    4    5   NA #> [3,]    7    8    9 mat |> mean_n(min_valid = 2) #> [1] 1.5 4.5 8.0

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests null hypothesis association (Pearson chi-squared test).

title: Phi coefficient — phi
text: phi coefficient \(\phi = \sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\). equivalent Cramer's V 2x2 tables equals Pearson correlation two binary variables. confidence interval uses Fisher z-transformation. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

text: Internal print method used freq() display styled, spicy-formatted frequency table console. formats valid, missing, total rows; handles cumulative valid percentages; appends labeled footer including metadata variable label, class, dataset name, weighting information.

title: Styled print method for freq() tables — print.spicy_freq_table

title: Styled print method for freq() tables — print.spicy_freq_table
text: x data.frame returned freq() attached attributes: "digits": number decimal digits display "data_name": name source dataset "var_name": name variable "var_label": variable label, defined "class_name": original class variable "weighted", "rescaled", "weight_var": weighting metadata ... Additional arguments (ignored, required S3 method compatibility)

title: Styled print method for freq() tables — print.spicy_freq_table
text: Invisibly returns x printing formatted table.

title: Styled print method for freq() tables — print.spicy_freq_table
text: function part spicy table rendering engine. automatically called printing result freq() styled = TRUE. output uses spicy_print_table() internally render colorized ASCII table consistent alignment separators. printed table includes: Valid missing value sections (applicable) Optional cumulative valid percentages final 'Total' row shown Category column footer summarizing metadata (variable label, data source, weights)

title: Styled print method for freq() tables — print.spicy_freq_table
text: printed table includes following columns: Category: Sections "Valid", "Missing", "Total" Values: Observed categories levels Freq.: Frequency count (weighted applicable) Percent: Percentage total Valid Percent: Percentage among valid values (optional) Cum. Percent: Cumulative percentage (optional) Cum. Valid Percent: Cumulative valid percentage (optional)

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: somers_d() computes Somers' D two-way contingency table ordinal variables.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
code: somers_d(   x,   direction = c("row", "column", "symmetric"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "row" (default, column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column), "symmetric" (average directions). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Somers' D — somers_d
text: Somers' D asymmetric ordinal measure defined \(d = (C - D) / (C + D + T)\), \(T\) number pairs tied independent variable. symmetric version harmonic mean two asymmetric values. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Somers' D — somers_d
code: tab <- table(sochealth$education, sochealth$self_rated_health) somers_d(tab, direction = "row") #> [1] 0.2015369 somers_d(tab, direction = "column", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper        p #>    0.208     0.157     0.258  < 0.001

what: spicy: Descriptive Statistics, Summary Tables, and Data Management Tools — spicy-package
title: spicy: Descriptive Statistics, Summary Tables, and Data Management Tools — spicy-package
text: Provides tools descriptive data analysis, variable inspection, data management, tabulation workflows 'R'. Summarizes variable metadata, labels, classes, missing values, representative values, support readable frequency tables, cross-tabulations, association measures contingency tables (Cramer's V, Phi, Goodman-Kruskal Gamma, Kendall's Tau-b, Somers' D, others), categorical continuous summary tables, including APA-style reporting outputs. Includes helpers interactive codebooks, variable label extraction, clipboard export, row-wise descriptive summaries. Designed make descriptive analysis faster, clearer, easier work practice.

title: spicy: Descriptive Statistics, Summary Tables, and Data Management Tools — spicy-package

what: Print a spicy-formatted ASCII table — spicy_print_table
title: Print a spicy-formatted ASCII table — spicy_print_table
text: User-facing helper prints visually aligned, spicy-styled ASCII table created functions freq() cross_tab(). automatically adjusts column alignment, spacing, separators improved readability console outputs. function wraps internal renderer build_ascii_table(), adding optional titles, notes, automatic alignment rules depending type table.

title: Print a spicy-formatted ASCII table — spicy_print_table
code: spicy_print_table(   x,   title = attr(x, "title"),   note = attr(x, "note"),   padding = c("compact", "normal", "wide"),   first_column_line = TRUE,   row_total_line = TRUE,   column_total_line = TRUE,   bottom_line = FALSE,   lines_color = "darkgrey",   align_left_cols = NULL,   align_center_cols = integer(0),   group_sep_rows = integer(0),   ... )

title: Print a spicy-formatted ASCII table — spicy_print_table
text: x spicy_table data.frame printed. title Optional title displayed table. Defaults "title" attribute x present. note Optional note displayed table. Defaults "note" attribute x present. padding Character string controlling horizontal spacing columns: "compact" - minimal spacing "normal" - moderate spacing (default) "wide" - extra spacing (wide displays) first_column_line Logical. TRUE (default), adds vertical separator first column. row_total_line, column_total_line, bottom_line Logical flags controlling presence horizontal lines total rows/columns bottom table. row_total_line column_total_line default TRUE; bottom_line defaults FALSE. lines_color Character. Color table separators. Defaults "darkgrey". applied output supports ANSI colors (see crayon::has_color()). align_left_cols Integer vector column indices left-align. NULL (default), alignment auto-detected based x: freq tables -> c(1, 2) cross tables -> 1 align_center_cols Integer vector column indices center-align. Defaults integer(0). group_sep_rows Integer vector row indices light dashed separator line drawn. Defaults integer(0). ... Additional arguments passed build_ascii_table().

title: Print a spicy-formatted ASCII table — spicy_print_table
text: Invisibly returns x, printing formatted ASCII table console.

title: Print a spicy-formatted ASCII table — spicy_print_table
text: spicy_print_table() detects whether table represents frequencies (freq-style) cross-tabulations (cross-style) adjusts formatting accordingly: frequency tables, first two columns (Category Values) left-aligned. cross tables, first column (row variable) left-aligned. function supports Unicode line-drawing characters colored separators using crayon package, graceful fallback monochrome output color supported. table exceeds console width, split stacked horizontal panels repeating left-identifier columns.
